other_observers: None; One of these birds was probably observed the previous day by John and Jan Rees, Susan Allerton, and a fourth observer.
description: Birds were about the size of a Snowy Egret and were on the slightly larger than the accompanying White-faced Ibises. Body coloring was dark brown, that appeared slightly richer (more rurous) around the neck and head. Flight feathers were irridescent with tones of bronze and greenish metallic. Bill was long and decurved and brownish-gray. Legs were dark grayish-brown with distinctive reddish taurus joints that were conspicuous, especially when compared by accompanying WFIB. The most distinctive parts of the birds were the powder blue facial skin extending from the bill to the front of the eye which was edged with white. The facial skin ended at the front of the eye and did not extend over, under, or around the eye. The eye appeared black.Behaviors: One bird was seen at rest with about 250 other plegadis ibises. It was preening while I observed it allowing me to closely observe the bird's head from several angles. Also, since it was at the near edge of the flock the legs were clearly visible.  The second bird was several hundred yards away from the first, and was actively feeding with a group of WFIB. This individual approached to within about 75 feet of me while I observed in through my 32x scope providing me with exceptional views, ost of which were side or head-on.Call: Not heardPlumage: alternate
